questions.q: In NVIDIA's SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Thread) model, what is a warp?
options: A data structure that stores branch prediction information
A group of 32 threads that execute the same instruction in lockstep on a streaming multiprocessor
A memory access pattern that exploits coalescing for bandwidth efficiency
A technique for dynamically reassigning threads to different cores
questions.explanation: A warp is the fundamental unit of execution in NVIDIA GPUs—32 threads that execute the same instruction in parallel. SIMT allows conditional execution through masking: threads with different branch outcomes are masked off, but all threads in a warp still execute both paths sequentially. This is key to GPU efficiency.

questions.q: What is occupancy in GPU computing?
options: The percentage of SIMD lanes in use during execution
The ratio of active warps to the maximum number of warps that can reside on a streaming multiprocessor
The frequency at which threads access memory relative to compute operations
The power consumption of the GPU relative to its peak rated capacity
questions.explanation: Occupancy measures how many warps are concurrently resident on each SM (streaming multiprocessor). High occupancy (e.g., 75%) means the SM can hide latency effectively by switching between active warps. Low occupancy indicates underutilized hardware or register/memory pressure limiting warp residency.

questions.q: In a GPU's memory hierarchy, what is the purpose of shared memory (also called local memory on AMD)?
options: To cache data from global memory for all threads on the GPU
To provide fast, on-chip memory shared by threads within the same thread block
To store constant values and kernel arguments for the entire grid
To back up register values when register pressure is high
questions.explanation: Shared memory is fast on-chip SRAM (typically 48-96 KB per SM on NVIDIA) shared by threads in a thread block. It has much lower latency than global memory and enables efficient data sharing within a block. It's programmer-controlled, unlike caches.

questions.q: Why do GPUs require coalesced memory accesses for good performance?
options: Coalescing reduces the number of floating-point operations needed
Threads in a warp access memory together; uncoalesced access requires multiple transactions, wasting bandwidth
Coalesced addresses are always in cache, eliminating misses
Global memory is actually faster than shared memory if accesses are coalesced
questions.explanation: Threads in a warp should access consecutive addresses so a single 128-byte transaction (or cache line) satisfies multiple threads. Uncoalesced access (e.g., stride-32 pattern on 32 threads) requires 32 separate transactions, drastically reducing effective bandwidth. Coalescing is a key optimization in GPU programming.

questions.q: A kernel experiences warp divergence when threads within a warp take different code paths due to an if-else branch. What is the performance impact?
options: Threads on the inactive path are discarded and lost
Both paths are executed sequentially in the warp, with threads masked to inactive branches, reducing throughput
The warp is automatically split into sub-warps that execute independently
Divergence has no impact if the shared memory is sufficient
questions.explanation: On GPUs, branch divergence causes both paths to execute in sequence with threads masked off on the inactive path. This serializes the warp, reducing throughput. In the worst case, if all 32 threads diverge, the kernel runs 32 times slower than optimal. Minimizing divergence is critical for GPU performance.

questions.q: What role does a streaming multiprocessor (SM) play in a GPU architecture?
options: It stores the entire global memory for the GPU
It is the basic unit of execution, containing cores, caches, shared memory, and warp schedulers
It schedules CPU threads to run on the GPU
It acts as a backup for failed GPU cores
questions.explanation: An SM (or compute unit on AMD) is the GPU's execution unit. It contains multiple cores, registers, shared memory, instruction caches, and warp schedulers. A GPU may have 10-80+ SMs. Multiple warps execute on the same SM, with the scheduler hiding latency by switching between them.

questions.q: In GPU register allocation, what is the effect of using too many registers per thread?
options: Registers are unlimited; using more has no impact
Higher register usage reduces the number of warps that can reside on each SM, decreasing occupancy and latency hiding
Registers are allocated from global memory, so excess register use increases memory bandwidth
Using more registers automatically increases compute throughput
questions.explanation: Each thread has a limited number of registers (typically 255 per thread in modern NVIDIA GPUs). If a kernel uses many registers, fewer warps fit on each SM, reducing occupancy. Lower occupancy means fewer warps to hide latency, hurting performance. Register optimization is critical for achieving high occupancy.

questions.q: What is the difference between local memory and shared memory in NVIDIA's CUDA programming model?
options: Local memory is faster and shared among threads in a thread block; shared memory is global and accessible by all blocks
Local memory is per-thread memory spilled to global memory (slow); shared memory is fast on-chip SRAM shared by threads in a block
There is no difference; the terms are synonymous
Local memory is for integer operations; shared memory is for floating-point
questions.explanation: Shared memory is a fast, programmer-controlled on-chip cache for threads in a block. Local memory is per-thread, but when registers overflow, it spills to slow off-chip memory. Shared memory is ideal for inter-thread communication; local memory is automatically managed and should be minimized.

questions.q: How does a GPU's approach to branch prediction differ from a CPU's?
options: GPUs use more advanced branch predictors than CPUs
GPUs rely on warp divergence serialization rather than prediction; complex predictors add little value due to massive parallelism
GPUs disable branching to avoid any misprediction overhead
Branch prediction is identical on GPUs and CPUs
questions.explanation: Branch predictors provide little benefit on GPUs because latency is hidden by switching between many warps. When divergence occurs, both paths execute with masking. Modern GPUs use simple or no branch prediction, focusing on occupancy to hide divergence latency rather than speculative execution.

questions.q: A GPU kernel with 1024 threads per thread block and 96 KB of shared memory per SM runs on hardware with 48 KB per SM. What will happen?
options: The kernel will fail to compile due to register mismatch
The kernel will be compiled successfully, and 32 threads will share allocated space, reducing occupancy
The kernel will dynamically increase SM shared memory at runtime
Shared memory allocation exceeds available capacity; the kernel will not launch or will fail at runtime
questions.explanation: Each thread block requires 96 KB of shared memory, but only 48 KB is available per SM. The kernel cannot launch successfully. The programmer must either reduce per-block shared memory usage or reduce threads per block. This is a hard limit enforced at kernel launch time.
